demographics: I am 35 years old. I am male. I am White. I have never been married. I had 2 years of college education. My family income is $90,000 to $109,999. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am moderate (middle of the road). I have no religious affiliation. I attend religious services about once or twice a year.

events.event_text: I was laid off from my construction job last year, after working there for over a decade. It was a tough time, but myployer's religious community rallied around me, helping with funds and emotional support until I found a new job.

When I was 22, I adopted a rescue dog, a scrawny and scared lab mix named Max. Caring for him helped me cope with the stress of my first job out of college. It was a humbling experience, and I learned how much love and patience can transform a life.

When I was 28, I organized a food drive for our local homeless shelter as part of a community service project for my church. It was humbling to see how the small actions of a group can make such a big difference, and it opened my eyes to the struggles faced by people in my own community.

When I was 25, I witnessed the wrongful termination of a coworker due to his sexuality. The experience left a bitter taste in my mouth, and I became more vocal about workplace discrimination, even testifying in front of a local city council to advocate for better protections.

At 29, I was laid off from my construction job after a decade. It was tough, but my former foreman, who's part of a local church, pulled me aside and offered me a place to stay in the community's housing while I got back on my feet. They even helped me find my current job.

At 30, I started volunteering at a local community center, teaching construction skills to underprivileged youth. I've witnessed their growth and potential, and it's been incredibly rewarding to see them gain confidence and skills that will help them build better lives for themselves.

At 27, I was arrested and spent a night in jail after protesting for better worker protections. It was terrifying, but it also opened my eyes to the reality of our criminal justice system and the privileges I have that others don't.

At 21, I moved out of my parent's house and into a high-diversity neighborhood to save money. It was a culture shock at first, but I've made friends from all walks of life, learning about their experiences and challenges. It's opened my eyes to how different life can be for others.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person experienced a decade-long career in construction, followed by a layoff and subsequent support from their employer's religious community. They adopted a rescue dog, Max, at 22, which helped them cope with post-college stress and taught them about love and patience. At 28, they organized a food drive, witnessing the impact of community action, and at 25, they witnessed workplace discrimination, becoming an advocate for better protections.

This person, at 29, faced job loss and homelessness, but found support in a local church community. At 30, they began volunteering at a community center, teaching construction skills to underprivileged youth, witnessing their growth and potential. At 27, they were arrested during a protest for worker protections, experiencing the criminal justice system firsthand.

persona_card:
**Persona Card:**

This 35-year-old, college-educated, independent male, with a moderate religious affiliation, is a blend of traditional and progressive values. He believes in a limited role for government in economic redistribution, yet advocates for worker protections and has witnessed the impact of community action. While he holds structural explanations for racial inequality, his trust in institutions is low, shaped by personal experiences with the criminal justice system. Despite his moderate outgroup affect, he is open and welcoming towards diverse social groups, having found support in various communities during challenging times.
